definitions.example: The set of natural numbers is denoted by N = {1, 2, 3, ...}.
definitions.definition: A set is a well-defined collection of unique objects, called elements or members, which can be anything (numbers, letters, people, etc.).

definitions.example: In the set {1, 2, 3}, 1 and 2 are elements.
definitions.definition: An element is a member of a set. It is an individual object within a set.
A subset is a set whose elements are all elements of another set. It is denoted by ⊆.
A proper subset is a subset that is not equal to the original set. It is denoted by ⊂.

definitions.example: If we are considering the set of students in a school, the universal set might include all students, teachers, and staff.
definitions.definition: The universal set is the set that contains all elements of a particular context.
An empty set is a set with no elements.

definitions.example: {} is also known as the null set.
definitions.definition: A null set is another name for the empty set.
The union of two or more sets is a set containing all elements from the individual sets.
The intersection of two or more sets is a set containing all elements common to the individual sets.
The complement of a set is the set of all elements in the universal set that are not in the given set.
A finite set is a set with a finite number of elements.

definitions.example: The set of natural numbers is an infinite set.
definitions.definition: An infinite set is a set with an infinite number of elements.
The cardinality of a set is the number of elements in the set.
Two sets are equivalent if they have the same number of elements.
Two sets are disjoint if they have no elements in common.
The power set of a set is the set of all possible subsets of that set.

definitions.example: The Cartesian product of {1, 2} and {a, b} is {(1, a), (1, b), (2, a), (2, b)}.
definitions.definition: The Cartesian product of two sets is the set of all ordered pairs formed by taking one element from each set.
